chunk_summary: This section discusses risk-stratified introduction of peanut and egg in infants starting at six months, emphasizing allergy prevention strategies based on guidelines from NIAID, AAP, and ASCIA.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of introducing allergenic foods like peanut and egg at six months to prevent allergies.
Learn risk-stratified approaches for introducing allergenic foods based on infant health and family history.
Familiarize with guidelines from NIAID, AAP, and ASCIA for allergy prevention in infants.
Identify safe practices for introducing solid foods to infants under two years.
key_facts: Peanut introduction is recommended as early as 4-6 months for high-risk infants (NIAID guidelines).
Egg and peanut introduction should be done in small amounts and monitored for allergic reactions (AAP guidance).
ASCIA recommends introducing solid foods, including allergens, by six months to reduce allergy risk.
High-risk infants include those with severe eczema or egg allergy, requiring consultation with a specialist before introducing peanuts.
Early introduction of allergenic foods has been shown to reduce the risk of developing food allergies by up to 80% in some cases.
reference_quote: For high-risk infants, peanut-containing foods should be introduced as early as 4-6 months, following evaluation by a healthcare provider. (NIAID guidelines)
document_key_topics: Complementary feeding starting at six months
Guidelines for peanut and egg allergy prevention
